title: How does retrieval layer quality directly affect model output quality in a RAG system?
prompt:
How does retrieval layer quality directly affect model output quality in a RAG system?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution: The model generates responses based primarily on what retrieval provides — the generation quality ceiling is set directly by retrieval quality. The model can only work with what's inside its context window: wrong context produces a wrong answer, and no relevant context produces either a hallucination or an unhelpful "I don't know." How retrieval failures manifest: a recall failure (missing doc) means the model lacks required facts and either refuses or hallucinates plausible-sounding text; a precision failure (irrelevant doc retrieved) causes the model to synthesize a confusing, blended, misleading answer from contradictory context; stale doc retrieval causes the model to confidently answer based on outdated policy. Why it's easy to miss: retrieval errors look exactly like "model hallucinations" if you only inspect outputs — you must log retrieved chunk IDs and scores alongside every response to diagnose the actual root cause.
hint_notes: Generation quality is capped by retrieval quality. Recall failure → hallucination/refusal, precision failure → blended misleading answer, staleness → confidently wrong answer. Always log retrieved chunk IDs.
